1: Josiah was eight years old at his accession — the second child-king after Joash (2 Kings 11:21), who also acceded to the throne after an assassination. The eight-year-old king will grow into the most faithful king of the Davidic line. The regnal length — thirty-one years — is the middle length, neither the extreme of Manasseh’s fifty-five nor the brevity of Amon’s two. Josiah’s mother’s name, Yᵉḏîḏāh (“beloved of YHWH”), is programmatic: the beloved king’s mother carries the divine epithet.
2: “He did what was right in the sight of YHWH and followed completely the way of his ancestor David, not turning aside to the right or to the left.” The phrase “not turning aside right or left” (lōʾ sār yāmîn ûśmōʾl) is the Deuteronomic formula for perfect covenant fidelity (Deuteronomy 5:32; 2 Kings 17:20; Joshua 1:7). It is applied only to David and Josiah in the entire Deuteronomistic History — the two kings who fully embody covenant faithfulness. The comparison is to “his ancestor David” (kᵉkol derek dāwîḏ ʾāḇîyw) — not merely “as David did” but “in the complete way of David.”
3: In Josiah’s eighteenth year he sends Shaphan to the temple for the repair. The eighteenth year (621 BCE) is the theological pivot of the narrative — the year of the book’s discovery. Shaphan (šāpān — rock hyrax) becomes one of the most important figures in the late monarchic period: he and his family (Ahikam, Gedaliah) repeatedly protect prophets and reformers (Jeremiah 26:24; 2 Kings 36:10-12; 2 Kings 40:5-6). The sōpēr (secretary/scribe) was a senior state official with both administrative and literary responsibilities.
4: Hilkiah the high priest (hakkōhēn haggādôl) is to count the money brought to the temple — temple income from donors channeled through doorkeepers. The administrative machinery of Josiah’s reform is organized: money gathered, supervisors appointed, workers paid. The repair of the temple is the physical analog to the spiritual repair Josiah will undertake — both require systematic attention to what has been neglected.
5: The repair supervisors (pəqûḏê habbayit) are to pay the workers. The temple workers are ʿōśê hāmᵉlāʾkāh — “those who do the work” — the same phrase used for the craftsmen of the Mosaic tabernacle (Exodus 36:2, 4, 8). The terminology links Josiah’s temple repair to the original construction of Israel’s sacred dwelling.
6: Carpenters (ḥārāšîm), builders (bōnîm), stonemasons (gōdᵉrê ḥômaʿ), timber (ʿēṣ), and dressed stone (ʾabnê māḥṣēḇ). The listing of specific building trades and materials reflects the administrative precision of the reform project. The Deuteronomistic historian includes the detail to establish that the temple repair was real and systematic, not a symbolic gesture.
7: “They were not required to keep accounts for the money entrusted to them, because they were acting honestly (ʾĕmet).” ʾĔmet — faithfulness/reliability/truth — is the term for covenantal integrity (Exodus 18:21; Psalms 15:2). The workers’ ʾĕmet is what makes accounting unnecessary: where there is faithfulness, there is no need for oversight. The same principle operated in the earlier temple repair of Joash (2 Kings 12:15). The exemption from accounting is a literary marker of moral integrity.
8: Hilkiah announces to Shaphan: “I have found the sēfer hattôrāh — the Book of the Law — in the house of YHWH.” This is the most significant textual discovery in the OT narrative. The sēfer hattôrāh is widely identified in scholarship as some form of Deuteronomy, given that the subsequent reform precisely targets Deuteronomy’s prohibitions. The verb māṣāʾ — “found” — carries the covenantal resonance of “discovering what was lost.” Hilkiah gives the book to Shaphan; Shaphan reads it; then reads it to the king.
9: Shaphan’s report to the king covers two items: (1) the money has been disbursed to the supervisors and workers, and (2) Hilkiah has given him a book. The administrative item first, then the potentially explosive textual item — the sequence mirrors the administrative context in which the discovery was embedded. Temple repair finances led to the Torah’s rediscovery: the material act of restoration and the textual act are linked.
10: Shaphan reads the book aloud to the king — wayyiqrāʾhû ʿal pᵉnê hammelek, “read it before the face of the king.” The oral reading is the primary mode of Torah reception (Deuteronomy 31:11-13; Nehemiah 8:1-8): the king must hear the law, not merely possess it. The Torah is not a private text but a public, oral covenant document. The reading before the king anticipates the covenant-reading before all Israel in 2 Kings 23.
11: “When the king heard the words of the Book of the Law, he tore his clothes (wayyiqraʿ ʾet bᵉgādāyw).” The tearing of garments is the physical expression of grief and crisis: Jacob at Joseph’s supposed death (Genesis 37:34), David at Saul’s death (2 Samuel 1:11), Hezekiah at Sennacherib’s ultimatum (2 Kings 19:1). For Josiah, the grief is specifically covenantal: the hearing of the Torah’s curses in light of Israel’s manifest non-compliance produces the grief of a king who understands what the book means for his people.
12: Josiah dispatches a five-person delegation to “inquire of YHWH” (dərəšû ʾet YHWH) — the technical term for prophetic consultation (1 Kings 22:5, 8; Jeremiah 21:2). The delegation includes the high priest, a court official, the secretary, and the king’s attendant — senior representatives of temple, court, and administration. The fact that Josiah sends others rather than going himself is not evasion but protocol: the king sends delegations for oracular consultation.
13: “Inquire of YHWH for me, for the people, and for all Judah about what is written in this book that has been found. Great is YHWH’s anger that burns against us because our ancestors did not obey (šāmᵉʿû) the words of this book, to do according to everything written for us.” Josiah’s framing of the inquiry is remarkable: he immediately reads the book as a word of judgment already in process, not as a future threat. The “for us” (ʿālênû) shows that he identifies with his ancestors’ failure, not as a spectator. The šmʿ-failure (not obeying/listening) is the exact reverse of the šmʿ-command that Deuteronomy begins with: “Hear (šᵉmaʿ), O Israel.”
14: The delegation goes to Huldah the prophetess — hûlḏāh hannᵉḇîʾāh — wife of Shallum, keeper of the wardrobe, living in the Second District of Jerusalem. The choice of Huldah over the contemporary prophets Jeremiah (active from 627 BCE) and Zephaniah (active under Josiah) has generated scholarly discussion; the narrative offers no explanation. Huldah is one of four named female prophets in the OT (Miriam, Deborah, Huldah, Noadiah — Noadiah is a false prophet). Her oracle authenticates the book and determines Judah’s response to it. The “keeper of the wardrobe” (šōmēr habbᵉgādîm) — her husband’s title — indicates an inner-court official position.
15: “This is what YHWH, the God of Israel, says: Tell the man who sent you to me” — the standard prophetic messenger formula (kōh ʾāmar YHWH), indicating that Huldah speaks as YHWH’s authorized messenger. She does not speak in her own name but transmits the divine word. The referential “the man who sent you” (not “the king”) may reflect the formal distance of prophetic address or a deliberate de-emphasizing of royal status.
16: “I am going to bring disaster on this place and its people, according to everything written in the book the king of Judah has read.” Huldah confirms two things: (1) the book is authentic — it is YHWH’s word; (2) the judgment it announces is certain and will come. The “book the king of Judah has read” is the standard by which history is being measured, and the measurement finds the people wanting. Josiah’s reform, no matter how thorough, will not reverse the verdict — it will only determine the timing.
17: “Because they have abandoned me (ʿāzᵉḇûnî) and burned incense to other gods, provoking my anger (lᵉmaʿan hakhʿîsēnî) through everything their hands have done, my anger (ḥărātî) will burn against this place and will not be quenched (lōʾ tiḵbeh).” The ʿāzaḇ-ḥārāh-lōʾ tiḵbeh sequence — abandonment leading to anger that cannot be extinguished — is the covenant-curse vocabulary of Deuteronomy (Deuteronomy 29:20). The fire of divine anger echoes Elijah’s fire at Carmel, but this is the fire of judgment, not vindication.
18: The oracle pivots to address Josiah specifically: “As for the king of Judah who sent you to inquire of YHWH — say this to him.” The personal oracle for Josiah is separated from the national oracle by the structural marker “say this to him.” The distinction is crucial: collective judgment and individual exception occupy the same prophetic word simultaneously.
19: “Because your heart was responsive (wayyikkānēʿ lᵉḇāḇᵉkā) and you humbled yourself before YHWH when you heard what I spoke against this place and its people.” Kānāʿ — to humble/subdue — is used only twice in the Deuteronomistic History in this self-humbling sense: 1 Kings 21:29 of Ahab, and here of Josiah. Both humiliations temporarily defer divine judgment. The term carries the sense of a high thing brought low — the king who lowers himself before the word earns the word’s mercy. YHWH specifies what he heard: (1) tearing garments; (2) weeping before him. The physical acts of grief are the evidence of the heart’s response.
20: “Therefore I will gather you to your ancestors, and you will be buried in peace (bəšālôm). Your eyes will not see all the disaster I am going to bring on this place.” The promise of a peaceful death is the personal reward for Josiah’s humility. The word šālôm — wholeness/peace — is the opposite of the curse. Josiah will be gathered to his ancestors before the catastrophe. The irony that follows in 2 Kings 23:29-30 is sharp: Josiah dies in battle at Megiddo, which seems inconsistent with “buried in peace.” The rabbinic and Christian interpretation is that “in peace” means before the exile — he does not live to see Jerusalem fall, which is the peace promised.
